coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and characterization of Ni@UiO-66 Metal-Organic Framework for fluorescence detection of heavy metal ions in the aqueous phase
coredata.dc:creator: Ansari, Amad Ashraf
Khan, Amjad Mumtaz
Salem, Mansour A.S.
Bhat, Aadil Shafi
coredata.dc:description: The toxicity of clean fresh water has become a major concern in the 21st century. The pollution of fresh and marine water bodies via anthropogenic activities introduces fatal chemicals like heavy metal ions into the environment causing disastrous impacts on human life as well as floral-faunal biota. Hence, it is crucial to develop a nanomaterial that can probe on these lethal heavy metal ions feasibly as well as with low detection limits. Metal-Organic Frameworks are frequently employed in optics as fluorescence sensors for detecting heavy metal ions. Therefore, in this study, Ni(II) was doped into the UiO-66 network, and the fluorescent characteristics of the obtained MOF structure were observed. The morphological characterizations were conducted via PXRD and SEM while the optical tests based on fluorescent spectroscopy were given more attention. The as synthesized MOF i.e., Ni@UiO-66 gave excellent fluorescent detection exhibiting “Turn-Off” for Hg(II) and Fe(III), while “Turn On” for Cr(VI), and Al(III) analytes. In aqueous phase, the lowest detection limit values of 7.98 x 10−7 M for Hg(II) ions is observed. The former's sensing mechanism was attributed to the inner filter effect (IFE), while the latter's mechanism could potentially be linked to the chelation-enhanced fluorescent (CHEF) effect. These findings imply that the Ni@UiO-66 metal-organic framework has the potential to be used for the detection for minute quantities of Hg(II), Al(III), Cr(VI), and Fe(III) ions.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and characterization of Ni@UiO-66 Metal-Organic Framework for fluorescence detection of heavy metal ions in the aqueous phase

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The toxicity of clean fresh water has become a major concern in the 21st century. The pollution of fresh and marine water bodies via anthropogenic activities introduces fatal chemicals like heavy metal ions into the environment causing disastrous impacts on human life as well as floral-faunal biota. Hence, it is crucial to develop a nanomaterial that can probe on these lethal heavy metal ions feasibly as well as with low detection limits. Metal-Organic Frameworks are frequently employed in optics as fluorescence sensors for detecting heavy metal ions. Therefore, in this study, Ni(II) was doped into the UiO-66 network, and the fluorescent characteristics of the obtained MOF structure were observed. The morphological characterizations were conducted via PXRD and SEM while the optical tests based on fluorescent spectroscopy were given more attention. The as synthesized MOF i.e., Ni@UiO-66 gave excellent fluorescent detection exhibiting “Turn-Off” for Hg(II) and Fe(III), while “Turn On” for Cr(VI), and Al(III) analytes. In aqueous phase, the lowest detection limit values of 7.98 x 10−7 M for Hg(II) ions is observed. The former's sensing mechanism was attributed to the inner filter effect (IFE), while the latter's mechanism could potentially be linked to the chelation-enhanced fluorescent (CHEF) effect. These findings imply that the Ni@UiO-66 metal-organic framework has the potential to be used for the detection for minute quantities of Hg(II), Al(III), Cr(VI), and Fe(III) ions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Water is a major source of life for all living beings on the planet but due to the increase in anthropogenic activities in the 21st century, water pollution has become a severe problem for developed as well as underdeveloped nations with over 2 billion people in stressed areas [1,2]. As we have a finite amount of useable freshwater available, this problem puts life sustainability on Earth in jeopardy. As per the surveys, 70 % of groundwater in India is contaminated and 80 % of pollution in marine waters originates on land containing organic chemicals, heavy metals, agricultural fertilizers, etc. [3,4]. These contaminants are introduced into the environment by chemical industry sewage, agricultural disposals, thermal power plants, mining, leather tanneries, and sewers of cities and towns whether placed around shores or at far inland [5].

Heavy metal accumulation in the water bodies from these human activities causes a serious threat to human health and biota even at lower concentrations. An excessive amount of Mercury (Hg (II)), Chromium (Cr (VI)), Aluminum (Al (III)), Iron (Fe (III)), etc. can endanger living ecosystems through bioaccumulation in the food chain. In addition to impairing blood composition, heavy metals' presence in the human body can lead to reduced energy levels, disruption of cognitive functions, and impact the proper functioning of various organs such as the kidney, liver, lungs, and brain [6]. Heavy metal exposure can impair physical, neurological, and muscle function if it persists. Moreover, heavy metals can give rise to illnesses like muscular dystrophy (MD), multiple sclerosis, Parkinson's disease, and Alzheimer's disease [7]. Some heavy metals and their compounds may potentially cause cancer if they are exposed over time. To prevent this potential threat, the WHO has decreed minimum standards for the content of heavy metals in drinking water [8].

Considering the significant consequences of heavy metals on both human well-being and the environment, it becomes imperative to create sensing agents that can accurately and precisely detect these heavy metals while maintaining low detection limits. Currently, methods like inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES), neutron activation analysis, atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), and similar techniques are commonly employed for detection. However, their widespread use is constrained by their high cost and the need for skilled instrument operation, thereby limiting their practicality [9]. Therefore, it is crucial to provide quick, affordable, and on-site detection techniques for highly accurate heavy metal ion detection.

In this respect, the use of porous nanomaterials for environmental remediation are widely used as adsorbents and photocatalysts for heavy metals, organic dyes, as well as iodine capture [10–12]. The use of these MOFs as a fluorescent probe is considered a feasible approach to fabricate a guest-host system encapsulated with guest moieties having desired optical, and electrochemical properties [13–18]. Metal-Organic Frameworks (MOFs) are being seen as a concerned nanomaterial due to their structural diversity, adjustable pore size, large surface area, and diverse functional sites which can be applied in chemical sensing, drug delivery, batteries, catalysis, etc [19]. Owing to these properties, MOFs can be used to enclose foreign elements by in-situ pre-as well as post-synthetic alterations to create dual-emissive fluorescence detectors with enhanced signal responsiveness [20–24]. For example, P. Jia et al. synthesized Ru@UiO-66-NH2 by in-situ encapsulation of Ru(bpy)3 2+ into UiO-66-NH2 for the detection of Hg2+ [25].

Herein, we have synthesized and characterised UiO-66 in its pristine form as well as modified it with Ni+2 cation to form Ni@UiO-66. Furthermore, the synthesized UiO-66 and Ni@UiO-66 materials are employed in the detection of heavy metal ions using the fluorescence determination method.

Sisco Research Laboratories Pvt. Ltd. and S D Fine-Chem Limited supplied Zirconium oxychloride (ZrOCl2·8H2O), 1,4-benzenedicarboxylic acid (H2BDC), Nickel nitrate hexahydrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O), N’N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) ((CH3)2NCHO), and ethyl alcohol (C2H6O). No additional purification was performed on any of the chemicals or materials utilized, all of which were of standard analytical quality. Double deionised water (DDW) was used as a solvent in the studies.

Zirconium oxychloride (ZrOCl2·8H2O) salt, 3.22 g or 10.00 mmol, was mixed in 50 mL of DMF and agitated for 30 min. Then the linker solution was made by mixing 1.66 g of 1,4-benzendicarboxylic acid (H2BDC) in 50 mL of DMF and stirred for 30 min. A 1:1 M concentration of the metal salt solution was added dropwise to the linker solution and agitated for 24 h. Then, the mixed solution was kept for 24 h in a 120 °C oven to obtain the precipitate. The precipitate was centrifuged for 30 min at 2500 rpm, and was subjected to three rounds of washing with both DMF and ethanol. Subsequently, the precipitate, once filtered, was dried in an oven at 60 °C, and the resulting powdered metal-organic framework was measured for its weight. Its structural formula is believed to be Zr6O4(OH)4[C6H4(CO2)2]12 [26].

The Ni@UiO-66 fluorescent probe was synthesized via a facile solvothermal process. In detail, H2BDC, ZrOCl2·8H2O, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O and DMF were blended in the molar ratio of 1:1:1:50, respectively. 3.22 g/10.00 mmol of zirconium oxychloride octahydrate (ZrOCl2·8H2O) salt, 1.66 g/10 mmol of 1,4-benzendicarboxylic acid (H2BDC) and 2.90 g of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O were dissolved in 50 mL of DMF each. All three solutions were magnetically stirred for 30 min to get a clear homogenous solutions. Both ZrOCl2·8H2O and Ni(NO3)2·6H2O solutions were poured dropwise into linker H2BDC solution and stirred for 24 h. The precipitation was achieved by subjecting the mixture to a temperature of 120 °C in an oven for duration another 24 h. The resulting precipitate was then separated and purified by centrifugation, followed by washing with DMF to remove any excess H2BDC and with ethanol to eliminate any remaining DMF. Finally, synthesized MOF was vacuum-dried for final 24 h at the temperature of 60 °C [27]. The synthesis scheme for both UiO-66 and Ni@UiO-66 is depicted in Scheme 1 .

Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) was performed using a high-performance X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku MiniFlEX 600) with known voltages and current (40 kV and 50 mA). A Cu-sealed (Cu Kα) tube with a wavelength of 0.15406 nm was utilized, and the diffraction pattern was recorded over a 2θ range from 0.5° to 50°. The scanning was conducted in steps of 0.02° at a rate of 1°/min at a temperature of 25 °C, using open quartz sample holders. FT-IR measurements were carried out using a Perkin Elmer Spectrum two instrument. The recorded spectra of the samples contained infrared absorbance signals from water at approximately 1600 cm−1 and 3200 cm−1. Furthermore, structural analysis and imaging was performed via Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) Model No. JSM 6510LV Make JEOL, Japan.

Powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) analysis provides valuable insights into the crystalline structure of materials. UiO-66 exhibits a cubic lattice structure, as evidenced by the major peak of (111) at 2θ value of 7.374 having lattice parameter a = 20.747 Å. This peak, characteristic of cubic systems, confirms the successful synthesis of UiO-66 [20,28]. The absence of prominent peaks other than 2θ value of 7.09 shown in Fig. 1 indicates high crystallinity and purity. The crystalline size of UiO-66 and Ni@UiO-66 calculated via the Debye-Scherrer Equation ( eq. (1) )
1
D = K λ / β
cos
θ
where, D is particle size, K is Scherrer constant,
λ
is wavelength of X-Ray beam,
β
is full width half maxima (FWHM), and
θ
is Bragg angle. The calculated d spacing came to be 11.978 nm for UiO-66, reinforcing its structural stability. Ni@UiO-66 maintains the cubic lattice structure observed in UiO-66, as indicated by the major peak at 2θ = 7.0, matching peak at (111) of UiO-66. The presence of minor peaks at 25.045 and 43.225 suggests successful doping of Ni as depicted in Table S1. Notably, the peak at 43.225 corresponds to Ni(II), corelating with previous literature, affirming the incorporation of Ni into the UiO-66 framework [29,30]. Furthermore, for Ni@UiO-66 the peak intensity slightly decreases from 270.60 of UiO-66 to 266.35 justifying that Ni atoms have substituted Zr atoms forming an amorphous structure [31–33]. Lastly, the calculated d spacing of 11.978 nm for Ni@UiO-66 mirrors that of UiO-66, indicating structural stability post-doping.

The description of qualitative chemical analysis is obtained using FTIR spectra, depicted in Fig. 2 . Most of the peaks among the two MOFs i.e., UiO-66 and Ni@UiO-66 were concurrent to each other, indicating that incorporation of Ni(II) to UiO-66 does not affect its structural formation. For UiO-66, the characteristic peaks at 1585.26, 1508.03 and 1399.70 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of the CO bonds within the carboxylic acid found in H2BDC [34]. This band shows the presence of a metal-organic terephthalic acid coordinate bonding. The benzene ring exhibits typical frame vibrations, which are represented by the small bands at 1508.03 cm−1 and 1298.55 cm−1. The peaks at 745.59 cm−1 and 669.51 cm−1 can be attributed to the presence of –Zr-(μ3)O [28]. In the case of the Ni@UiO-66 MOF, the characteristic bands of UiO-66 are still present, but there is a shift in the wave number compared to other situations. The O–H stretching vibrations observed in the Ni@UiO-66 nanocomposite appear at a wave number of 3378.66 cm−1 [35]. Furthermore, the stretching vibration of CO in the binary and ternary systems is shifted to a lower wavenumber compared to that of UiO-66. Specifically, in UiO-66, the characteristic peaks were noted at 1585.26 cm−1, 1508.03 cm−1, and 745.59 cm−1 are shifted to 1584.88 cm−1, 1385.83 cm−1 for C–H stretching, and 747.10 cm−1 for CC in –Zr-(μ3)O linkers, respectively, in the case of Ni@UiO-66 [34]. These downward shifts in the wave numbers of the UiO-66 peaks are likely due to the interactions occurring between Ni(II) and UiO-66 within the MOF network. Furthermore, it's important to mention that the identifiable peak observed at 3382.50 cm−1 can be attributed to the O–H bonds stretching vibrations [27,36].

The SEM images revealed a noticeable difference in surface roughness between UiO-66 and Ni@UiO-66 as demonstrated in Fig. 3 (a-d). In the case of UiO-66, the surface appeared relatively smooth and uniform, with well-defined crystal faces. This is consistent with the characteristic morphology of UiO-66, which typically exhibits well-defined octahedral crystals. On the other hand, the surface of Ni@UiO-66 displayed a significant increase in roughness. Irregularities, nanoparticle agglomerates, and uneven features were observed, indicating that the incorporation of Ni(II) had led to modifications in the surface structure of the material. The roughness increase might be attributed to the deposition of Ni(II) and their distribution across the UiO-66 framework. The SEM images also provided evidence of a size increase in the Ni@UiO-66 particles with respect to the pristine UiO-66. The UiO-66 particles exhibited a relatively uniform size distribution, consistent with the original synthesis method. In contrast, the Ni@UiO-66 particles displayed a broader size distribution, with some particles appearing notably larger than the typical UiO-66 crystals. This size increase is consistent with the successful incorporation of Ni(II) onto the UiO-66 framework. The growth of the particles might be attributed to the nucleation and growth of Ni(II) on the surface of the UiO-66 crystals, resulting in the observed size variation. These observations provide valuable insights into the structural alterations that occur upon the introduction of Ni(II), which could have implications for the material's properties and potential applications. To analyse the presence of Ni+2 in the UiO-66 network EDX spectra of UiO-66 and Ni@UiO-66 are compared. It is clear from Fig. 3(e) and (f) that Ni+2 metal ion is incorporated to the UiO-66 metal organic framework with the weight % of 9.35 for Ni+2 and 13.31 for Zr+4 ions. While C and O weight % differ minorly for both the spectra indicating stable H2BDC linkage in both the materials [37–39].

Thermal gravimetric analyses (TGA) were conducted to assess the thermal stability of UiO-66 and Ni@UiO-66 as shown in Fig. 4 . Both materials exhibited three discernible stages of weight loss, as depicted in the accompanying figure. Initially, between 30 °C and 185 °C, a phase was observed marked by the desorption of physically adsorbed ethanol. Subsequently, spanning from 185 °C to 450 °C, the process involved the expulsion of dimethylformamide (DMF), dehydroxylation of ZrO, and NiO in case of Ni@UiO-66. The third stage, commencing at 450 °C and extending to 560 °C, encompassed the decomposition of UiO-66 due to the combustion of organic-linker molecules within the framework. Beyond this, in the fourth stage from 560 °C to 800 °C, there was no notable mass change, indicating the presence of the ZrO2 phase. The findings imply that Ni@UiO-66 maintains its thermal stability up to 500 °C [40–46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection performance analysis of Ni@UiO-66

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence readings of Ni@UiO-66 were assessed to investigate its potential use in detecting heavy metals like Fe(III), Al(III), Hg(II), and Cr(VI), as well as to gain insight into the underlying interaction mechanisms. The effects of concentrations of Ni@UiO-66 were studied for fluctuation in FL spectra. Prior to fluorescence spectroscopy, 2 ppm concentration solutions of Hg(II), Al(III), Cd(II), Cr(VI), Pb(II), Mn(II), As(III), and Fe(III) are prepared in water. On the other hand, Ni@UiO-66 solution is prepared by grinding and dissolving it in water, and then stirring it overnight [47]. The fluorescence intensity (FL) exhibits a direct correlation with the rise in Ni@UiO-66 concentration, reaching its maximum peak intensity at 0.5 mg ml−1 with stable readings from 1 to 5 min as showed in Fig. 6(b). The FL spectra of Ni@UiO-66 illustrated in Fig. 5 (a) shows the prominent emission readings at 344 nm. In comparison to UiO-66 in the same Figure, fluorescence spectrum of Ni@UiO-66 was 13.5 nm blue-shifted with a severe rise in peak intensity [20,48–50]. Fig. 8(a&b), and Fig. 11(a&b) show the effect on emission peaks after adding Hg(II), Fe(III), Cr(VI) and Al(III), respectively. In the case of Hg(II) and Fe(III), the peak intensity falls consecutively on further addition of ions by 100 μL while this was vice versa for Al(III) and Cr(VI) [21]. Also, the FL of UiO-66 tested with these four metal cations showed no notable fluctuation from the original readings of pristine UiO-66 as shown in Fig. S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Turn off” fluorescence probing of Fe(III) & Hg(II)
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent response of Ni@UiO-66 towards some heavy metals like Fe(III), Hg(II), Al(III), and Cr(VI) is recorded by adding solution of these heavy metal ions to homogenously dispersed aqueous solution of Ni@UiO-66 prepared at room temperature. In Fig. 5(d), the introduction of various other heavy metal ions is observed to have a less pronounced impact in comparison to Hg(II) and Fe(III). Furthermore, the selectivity of Fe(III) and Hg(II) is studied among various heavy metal ions [51]. The intensities were less impacted, but fell constantly with consecutive increment of Fe(III) and Hg(II), as seen in Fig. 8(a&b). This shows that Ni@UiO-66 has a higher specificity for Fe(III) and Hg(II) ions in contrast to others.

To further evaluate the sensitivity of Ni@UiO-66 towards Fe(III) and Hg(II), the fluorescent titration is carried out with distilled water. In a sensitivity study, concentrations spanning from 0.2 to 2 ppm of both these heavy metal ions were examined. Fig. 8(a&b) shows the initial fluorescence intensity at 1986 and 2081 for Hg(II) and Fe(III) ions, respectively. Subsequently, there is a consecutive decline to 1524 and 1180 with increasing concentrations of in both the metal ions, respectively. This steady drop in fluorescence intensities with increased concentrations of Fe(III) and Hg(II) can be indicated to the inner filter effect (IFE) [52,53]. According to the IFE phenomenon, the absorption bands of the quencher i.e., Fe(III) and Hg(II) should complementarily coincide with that of the emission and excitation bands of the fluorescent agent i.e., Ni@UiO-66. Fe(III) shows a distinct UV absorption band at 292 nm while Hg(II) at 400 nm, as demonstrated in Fig. 6 [54,55]. Meanwhile, the produced nanomaterial Ni@UiO-66 exhibits fluorescence emission and excitation bands at 340 and 270 nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. 6(a&b). This explains why the FL intensity decreases consecutively on addition of Fe(III) and Hg(II) to the Ni@UiO-66 solution [51,56,57].

The effectiveness of the quenching was studied by using the Stern-Volmer equation ( eq. (2) ) i.e.,

Furthermore, the selectivity of Ni@UiO-66 came to be inclined towards Hg(II) ions when tested in the presence of mixture of several cations like Cr(VI), Cd(II), Pb(II), Mn(II), As(III), and Fe(III). On the other hand, the fluorescence intensities of Ni@UiO-66 were also tested with different cations without the involvement of Hg(II). It was observed that the fluorescent intensities were relatively unaffected by the presence of a mixture of cations but experienced a substantial decrease upon the introduction of Hg(II) ions, as illustrated in Fig. 9
. This indicates that, in contrast to other frequent cations present in aqueous solutions, Ni@UiO-66 shows good selectivity for Hg(II). The Hg(II) intensity in the Ni@UiO-66 did not significantly alter in the current study when these metal ions were present.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Turn On” fluorescence probing of Cr(VI) and Al(III)
ce:section.ce:para: The coupling of Ni@UiO-66 with Cr(VI) and Al(III) is recorded through fluorescence titration conducted in an aqueous medium. The fluorescent intensity grows sequentially by adding Cr(VI) and Al(III), as seen in Fig. 10 (a and b). This could be believed to be chelation-enhanced fluorescence (CHEF) effect. The photo-induced electron transfer (PET) mechanism is required for the Ni@UiO-66-Al(III) and Ni@UiO-66-Cr(VI) complexes of a CHEF effect [58,59]. As depicted in Fig. 12 , initially, in the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process, excited photons trigger electrons in the unbound Ni@UiO-66 to transition from donor atoms’ lone pairs, such as the oxygen within fluorophores. This transition leads to the quenching of fluorescence. Conversely, the presence of coordinated Al(III) and Cr(VI) ions within the Ni@UiO-66 sensor decreases the ion pair energy, effectively nullifying the PET effect and giving rise to the chelation-enhanced fluorescent effect (CHEF). Consequently, the FL intensity of Ni@UiO-66 becomes responsive to changes in the concentrations of Al(III) and Cr(VI). The LOD values for Ni@UiO-66 came to be as low as 1.39 x 10−6 and 2.60 x 10−6 M for Al(III) and Cr(VI), as depicted in Fig. 11 (a&b), respectively [51,56,60,61].

A comparison study was conducted with prior works. LOD values are lower in our study in contrast to previously reported Hg(II), Cr(VI), Al(III), and Fe(III) sensors shown in Table 1 . The highly porous nature of UiO-66 metal organic framework incorporates Ni2+ into its structure enhancing its fluorescent capabilities for the detection of heavy metals. In previous literatures, different materials as shown in Table 1 used fluorescent approach but they significantly differed in composition and structure. Our material Ni@UiO-66 showed phenomenon like Inner Filter Effect (IFE), Photoelectron Transfer (PET), and Chelation Enhanced Fluorescent Effect (CHEF) which uniquely defines it from other mentioned metal organic frameworks. Hence, we may conclude that the Ni@UiO-66 nanocomposite has good sensing behaviour towards the latter discussed heavy metal ions.

In this work, Ni@UiO-66 was feasibly synthesized via a solvothermal process by economical methods. 3D nanostructures with excellent incorporation of Ni(II) into the UiO-66 network was obtained. The fabricated metal-organic framework possesses excellent fluorescent detection capabilities when tested, and showed a “Turn-Off” and “Turn-On” effect for Hg(II) & Fe(III) and Al(III) & Cr(VI), respectively. Furthermore, the minimum limit of detection (LOD) recorded by Ni@UiO-66 came to be 7.98 x 10−7 M for Hg(II) ions. In conclusion, the research indicates that the Ni@UiO-66 metal-organic framework holds promise as a potential choice for the detection of minute quantities of Hg(II), Cr(VI), Al(III), and Fe(III) ions.

Amad Ashraf Ansari: Visualization, Writing – original draft. Amjad Mumtaz Khan: Supervision. Mansour A.S. Salem: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Aadil Shafi Bhat: Data curation, Writing – review & editing.
